explainer: Malbec is a red grape originally from Cahors, France, now iconic in Argentina.
Tempranillo is Spain's noble red grape, the backbone of Rioja and Ribera del Duero.
Viognier is an aromatic white grape from the Rhone Valley, known for stone fruit and floral notes.
Pinot Grigio (Pinot Gris) is a white grape. In Italy it's crisp; in Alsace, richer.
Nebbiolo is the red grape behind Barolo and Barbaresco. Tannic, aromatic, age-worthy.
Sangiovese is the red grape of Chianti, Brunello di Montalcino, and much of central Italy.
Gruner Veltliner is Austria's signature white grape. Peppery, fresh, versatile.
Mourvedre is a dark, tannic red grape used in Southern Rhone and Bandol blends.
Garnacha (Grenache) is a red grape thriving in Spain and Southern France.
Albarino is a white grape from Rias Baixas in Galicia, Spain. Saline and aromatic.
Carmenere is a red Bordeaux grape now mostly grown in Chile, once thought to be Merlot.
Assyrtiko is a high-acid white grape from Santorini, Greece. Volcanic minerality.
Tannat is a bold red grape from Madiran, France, now also prominent in Uruguay.
Nero d'Avola is Sicily's leading red grape. Rich, dark-fruited, and warming.
Torrontes is Argentina's signature aromatic white grape.
Aglianico is an ancient red grape from southern Italy. Taurasi and Aglianico del Vulture.
Verdejo is a crisp white grape from Rueda, Spain.
Xinomavro is a tannic red grape from Naoussa, Greece. Often compared to Nebbiolo.
Petit Verdot is a late-ripening red Bordeaux blending grape. Adds color and spice.
Furmint is the primary white grape of Tokaj, Hungary. Used for both dry and sweet wines.
Chardonnay is the world's most popular white grape. From lean Chablis to rich Napa.
Cabernet Sauvignon is the king of red grapes. Thick-skinned, tannic, age-worthy.
Merlot is a soft, plummy red grape. Dominant on Bordeaux's Right Bank.
Sauvignon Blanc is a zesty white grape. Loire and Marlborough are its spiritual homes.
Riesling is a noble aromatic white grape. Germany, Alsace, and Australia excel with it.
Syrah (Shiraz) is a dark, peppery red grape. Northern Rhone and Barossa are benchmarks.
Pinot Noir is a thin-skinned red grape. Burgundy, Oregon, and New Zealand are its strongholds.
Chenin Blanc is a versatile white grape from the Loire. Dry, sparkling, or sweet.
Semillon is a golden-skinned white grape. Blended with Sauvignon Blanc in Bordeaux; ages magnificently in Hunter Valley.
Gewurztraminer is an intensely aromatic white grape. Lychee, rose, and Turkish delight.
Gamay is the red grape of Beaujolais. Light, fruity, best served slightly chilled.
Cabernet Franc is an aromatic red grape. Parent of Cabernet Sauvignon; excels in the Loire.
Muscat is one of the oldest grape families. The only grape that actually tastes like grapes.
Vermentino is a crisp white grape from Sardinia, Corsica, and the Ligurian coast.
Touriga Nacional is Portugal's finest red grape. The backbone of great Port and Douro reds.
Chablis is an appellation in Burgundy, France. The grape is Chardonnay.
Sancerre is an appellation in the Loire Valley. The grape is Sauvignon Blanc.
Rioja is a region in Spain. The primary grape is Tempranillo.
Chianti is a region in Tuscany, Italy. The grape is Sangiovese.
Champagne is a region in France. The grapes are Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier.
Bordeaux is a region in France. Key grapes: Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Cabernet Franc.
Burgundy is a region in France. The grapes are Pinot Noir (red) and Chardonnay (white).
Priorat is a region in Catalonia, Spain. Key grapes: Garnacha and Carinena.
Chateauneuf-du-Pape is an appellation in the Southern Rhone. Up to 13 grape varieties are permitted.
Barossa Valley is a region in South Australia, famous for bold Shiraz.
Hermitage is an appellation in the Northern Rhone. The grape is Syrah.
Mosel is a wine region in Germany. The primary grape is Riesling.
Pauillac is a commune in Bordeaux's Medoc. The grape is primarily Cabernet Sauvignon.
Stellenbosch is a wine region in South Africa. Known for Cabernet Sauvignon and blends.
Mendoza is Argentina's premier wine region. Famous for Malbec.
Willamette Valley is a region in Oregon, celebrated for Pinot Noir.
Tokaj is a historic wine region in Hungary. Known for botrytized sweet wines from Furmint.
Jerez (Sherry Triangle) is a region in Andalusia, Spain. Home to Sherry.
Etna is a volcanic wine region in Sicily. Key grapes: Nerello Mascalese and Carricante.
Cote-Rotie is an appellation in the Northern Rhone. The grape is Syrah, sometimes with Viognier.
Napa Valley is California's most famous wine region. Known for Cabernet Sauvignon.
Marlborough is New Zealand's largest wine region. Defines crisp, aromatic Sauvignon Blanc.
Barolo is a DOCG in Piedmont, Italy. The grape is Nebbiolo. Often called the 'King of Wines.'
Brunello di Montalcino is a DOCG in Tuscany. The grape is Sangiovese (locally called Brunello).
Wachau is a wine region in Austria along the Danube. Known for Gruner Veltliner and Riesling.
Sonoma is a diverse wine region in California. Excels with Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Zinfandel.
Central Otago is the world's southernmost wine region, in New Zealand. Known for Pinot Noir.
Maipo Valley is Chile's most prestigious wine region. Known for Cabernet Sauvignon.
Cotes du Rhone is a large appellation in the Rhone Valley. The grapes are blends of Grenache, Syrah, and Mourvedre.
Beaujolais is a region in southern Burgundy, France. The grape is Gamay.
Prosecco is both a DOC/DOCG region in Veneto AND was a grape name. The grape was officially renamed Glera in 2009.
Muscadet is an appellation in the Loire AND a common name for the grape Melon de Bourgogne.
Lambrusco is both a family of red grapes AND a wine style/region in Emilia-Romagna, Italy.
Montepulciano is a red grape (Montepulciano d'Abruzzo) AND a town in Tuscany (Vino Nobile di Montepulciano uses Sangiovese).
Malvasia is both a large family of grape varieties AND historically a place-name from Monemvasia, Greece.

Sherry is a wine style AND a place. It refers to the Jerez region in Spain. The grapes are Palomino, Moscatel, and Pedro Ximenez. People often treat it like a grape, but it's a fortified wine style named after its origin.
Madeira is a place AND a wine style. It's a Portuguese island that gives its name to the fortified wine made there from grapes like Tinta Negra, Sercial, Verdelho, and Bual.
Marsala is a place AND a wine style. It's a city in western Sicily that gives its name to the fortified wine produced there, used both for drinking and cooking.
Franciacorta is a place AND a wine style. It's a DOCG region in Lombardy, Italy, and also the name for the traditional-method sparkling wine made there.
Champagne is a region in northeastern France. The grapes are Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier.
Valpolicella is a wine region in Veneto, Italy. Uses Corvina, Rondinella, and Molinara grapes.
Soave is a DOC in Veneto, Italy. The primary grape is Garganega.
Cava is a Spanish DO for traditional-method sparkling wine. Main grapes: Macabeo, Xarel-lo, Parellada.
Bandol is an appellation in Provence, France. The star grape is Mourvedre.
Barbaresco is a DOCG in Piedmont, Italy. Like Barolo, the grape is Nebbiolo.
Vouvray is an appellation in the Loire Valley. The grape is Chenin Blanc, in dry, off-dry, and sparkling styles.
Graves is an appellation in Bordeaux. Both red (Cabernet, Merlot) and white (Sauvignon Blanc, Semillon) wines.
Port is a place AND a wine style. Named after the city of Porto, Portugal, it's a fortified wine made from Touriga Nacional, Tinta Roriz, and others in the Douro Valley.
Sauternes is a Bordeaux appellation for botrytized sweet wines. Grapes: Semillon, Sauvignon Blanc, Muscadelle.
Savennieres is a small Loire appellation for powerful dry Chenin Blanc.
Gigondas is a Southern Rhone appellation. Primarily Grenache-based reds.
Zinfandel is a red grape. Genetically identical to Primitivo (Italy) and Crljenak Kastelanski (Croatia).
Primitivo is a red grape from Puglia, Italy. Genetically identical to Zinfandel.
Pinotage is South Africa's signature red grape. A cross of Pinot Noir and Cinsault, created in 1925.
Blaufrankisch is a spicy red grape from Austria and Hungary (where it's called Kekfrankos).
